docs.text: # Welcome to the Runico Wiki **Magical runes for your learning.** Runico turns anything you're learning into flashcards and helps you practice them. There's no install and no account — it runs right in your browser. You recall the answer, reveal it to check yourself, then tap Continue or Pause . That's the whole loop. No confusing "how well did you remember?" grading scales. Brand new here? Start with Getting Started — it gets you from zero to your first study session in a couple of minutes. ▶ Open Runico in your browser — it's free and ready right now What can Runico do? Each feature has its own page with a short animated demo, step-by-step instructions, and a worked example. Pick whatever you're curious about: Feature In one sentence 📖 Studying Cards Recall, reveal, then Continue or Pause — no grading scales. ⏯️ Resuming Your Last Session One tap drops you back on the exact card you stopped on. 🗂️ Browsing and Practicing Navigate subjects → folders → topics, then practice any of them. 🌱 Organizing Your Library Make folders, rename, move, copy, and sort your topics. 🔀 Merging Libraries Fold another .runico file into this one — content and history, with duplicate handling. 📈 Tracking Your Progress See whether a topic is trending up, session by session. ✨ Adding Cards Drop in material and let AI draft cards — or paste your own. 🤖 Using OpenRouter Connect a key so AI drafts cards for you, billed per use. 💬 Using Your Own AI Draft cards for free with ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini — no key. 🖼️ Image-Occlusion Cards Hide labels on a diagram to study figures, anatomy, and maps. 🔎 Seeing the Source Open the original material, with the studied term highlighted. 🎨 Settings Choose a canvas theme and the language for your cards and app. Good to know 💾 Your Library File — everything you make lives in one .runico file you own. 🗑️ Trash and Recovery — deleted something by accident? Get it back. ❓ FAQ & Troubleshooting — common questions and fixes. 📚 Glossary — plain-English definitions of every term used here. The 30-second version Open Runico and create a new library (a file that holds all your cards). Add some cards — drop in a page of notes, a screenshot, or a textbook figure. Study — recall each answer, reveal it, then Continue or Pause. Come back any time — Runico remembers exactly where you stopped. That's it. Welcome aboard. ✨

docs.text: ✨ Adding Cards Drop in material, let Runico draft cards, then keep the ones worth studying. Looping demo · plays automatically You can turn almost anything into flashcards — a textbook page, a screenshot, your class notes, a diagram. You drop in the material, Runico reads it and drafts cards for you , and you review each one and keep only the good ones. There are three ways to get cards into a topic. Pick whichever suits you: Let AI draft them for you (needs a free OpenRouter key — see below) Use your own AI chat (no key needed) Write them by hand Before you start: the AI key (optional but recommended) To have Runico draft cards automatically, it needs to talk to an AI model through a service called OpenRouter . This takes one minute to set up (full guide: Using OpenRouter ): Make a free account at openrouter.ai and create an API key. In Runico, open Settings → AI card generation . Paste your key and click Save . (Click Test key to confirm it works.) Optionally pick a Generation model — Runico shows a rough "≈ N cards per $1" estimate so you can balance quality against cost. 🔒 Your key stays private. In the browser it's stored only on your device; in the desktop app it's encrypted in your operating system's keychain. It's never sent anywhere except OpenRouter. No key, or don't want one? No problem — skip to Use your own AI chat . 1 · Let AI draft cards for you Start the flow. In the browser , open a folder and click ➕ Add AI Cards (or Add more AI Cards on a topic's action card). You'll see a breadcrumb like New AI Cards in Biology › Cell Biology so you know where the cards will land. Name the topic. Answer "What are you learning?" with a name like Cell signaling . Drop in your source. Use the "Drop, paste, or browse" zone — an image, a chapter, a screenshot, or a page of notes. Once a file lands you'll see a thumbnail and a "ready to read" check. (Optional) Set what matters most. Drag to reorder priorities like Definitions , Labeled diagrams , Examples , Body prose so the AI focuses on what you care about. Click Begin . Runico works in the background, cycling through Reading → Understanding → Drafting cards → Choosing the best . You can even leave this screen — your cards will be waiting. Review each draft. For every card you get Remove , Edit , or Keep . Keep the good ones, drop the rest. Done. You'll see "5 cards added to Cell signaling," with View cards and Back home . 💡 The cards stay as drafts until you review them — your topic shows a "N new cards ready" badge until you do. 2 · Use your own AI (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini…) No OpenRouter key? You can still get AI-drafted cards using any chat assistant you already use. On the Add screen, choose Use my own AI : Runico shows a ready-made prompt . Click Copy prompt . Paste it into your AI chat (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini — anything). If your source is an image, attach the image in the chat too. The AI replies with card data. Copy its whole response. Back in Runico, paste the response into the box and click Use these cards . Review and keep, exactly like the automatic flow. There's even an optional second prompt for building image-occlusion cards from a figure — see Image-Occlusion Cards . Full walkthrough: Using Your Own AI . 3 · Write cards by hand You can always author cards yourself. When editing a card you choose its type: Type What it is Example Q&A A question on the front, an answer on the back. Q: What organelle makes ATP? → A: Mitochondrion Cloze A sentence with a blank you fill in. Wrap the blank in {{ }} . The {{nucleus}} holds the cell's DNA. Reversible A term ↔ definition pair you can study in both directions. Front: Mitochondrion · Back: the cell's powerhouse A worked example You snap a photo of a textbook page on cell signaling. You: Click Add AI Cards inside Cell Biology . Name it Cell signaling , drop in the photo, and tap Begin . After a few seconds, 6 draft cards appear. You Keep 5 and Remove 1 that's too trivial. Tap View cards — your new topic is ready to study . ✨ Related 🤖 Using OpenRouter — set up the key, choose a model, and what it costs. 💬 Using Your Own AI — draft cards for free with ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini. 🖼️ Image-Occlusion Cards — make cards that hide labels on a figure. 🎨 Settings — where the OpenRouter key and model live. 🔎 Seeing the Source — each card links back to the material it came from.

docs.text: 🗂️ Browsing and Practicing Navigate subjects, folders, and topics in a column browser, then practice any topic. Looping demo · plays automatically Your whole library lives in a column browser that drills down from broad to specific: subjects → folders → topics . Think of it like opening nested folders on your computer — each time you tap into one, a new column slides in to show what's inside. The three layers Runico organizes everything into a simple hierarchy: Subjects / folders — your broad areas, like Biology or History . Folders can nest as deep as you like (a folder inside a folder inside a folder…). Topics — the actual decks of cards, living inside the deepest folder. For example, Photosynthesis or Membrane transport . Cards — the individual flashcards inside a topic. 📚 Not sure what "topic" vs "folder" means? The Glossary has plain-English definitions. How to navigate Start at Everything — the top column shows your whole library and what's due today. Tap a folder — a new column slides in showing what's inside it. Keep drilling until you reach a topic . Tap the topic — it opens an action card (see below). On a phone, the columns scroll sideways and the one you just opened slides to the center. Practice the whole column with "Practice all" Every column has a Practice all button. It studies everything in that column at once — so you can practice a single topic, or a whole folder full of topics, in one session. Handy when you want a broad review. The action card (what a topic opens to) When you tap a topic, it opens an action card — a little summary panel with everything you can do: A small accuracy-trend sparkline at the top — a glance at how recent sessions are going. Stats — e.g. 22 cards · 4 due · last 9d ago . A resume hint — e.g. Paused at card 2 · 3 of 4 remaining . Actions: Continue / Begin practice → start studying. Open cards → see and edit the individual cards. View progress → the full accuracy chart over time. Add more AI Cards → grow the topic with new cards. A worked example You want to review cells. You: Open Biology → a column shows Cell Biology, Genetics, Ecology . Open Cell Biology → a column shows Organelles, Membrane transport, Mitosis, Photosynthesis . Tap Photosynthesis → its action card opens. Tap Continue practice → you're studying. ✨ Or, if you'd rather review all of Cell Biology at once, tap Practice all in the Cell Biology column instead. Want to rearrange things? Making folders, renaming, moving, copying, and sorting topics all happen right here in the browser — see Organizing Your Library . Related 🌱 Organizing Your Library — folders, rename, move, copy, sort. 📈 Tracking Your Progress — the full trend view. 📖 Studying Cards — the study loop.

docs.text: ❓ FAQ & Troubleshooting Quick answers to the questions beginners ask most. Getting started Do I need to install anything or make an account? No. Just open Runico in your browser — no install, no account, no sign-in. (A desktop app is available if you prefer one.) Is it free? Yes, the app is free and open source. The only thing that can cost money is AI card generation , if you choose to use it — that's billed by OpenRouter based on your own usage. Studying, browsing, and writing your own cards are entirely free. What does it cost to use AI? Whatever your chosen model costs through OpenRouter. Runico shows a rough "≈ N cards per $1" estimate next to each model so you can pick one that fits your budget — or skip AI entirely and use your own AI chat for free. My data and saving Where is my data stored? In a single .runico file on your own device — see Your Library File . Runico has no server that holds your cards. It says it can't auto-save / "you'll save with a download." Why? Your browser doesn't support save-in-place. Use Chrome or Edge (or the desktop app) for auto-save, or just keep saving by download. Nothing is lost either way. Details: Your Library File . How do I move my library to another computer or my phone? Copy the .runico file (e.g. via a cloud-drive folder) and Open it on the other device. Your theme and AI key are device-specific, so you'll set those again there. Can I combine two libraries into one? Yes. Open one, click 🔀 Merge , and pick the other — its folders, topics, cards, and study history fold in, with a step to resolve any duplicate cards. The file you merge from is left untouched. See Merging Libraries . Is my data private? Yes. Your library stays in your file on your device. Your OpenRouter key is stored locally (or encrypted in your OS keychain on desktop) and is only ever sent to OpenRouter when you generate cards. Studying There's no 1–5 rating after each card. Is that a bug? Nope — it's the whole idea. Runico keeps it to Continue or Pause , with no grading scale. See Studying Cards . Does checking my progress change what I study next? No. The progress charts are reporting only — looking never changes scheduling or what's due. I deleted something by accident! Open the Trash and click Restore . Deletes are recoverable until you empty the trash. AI cards AI generation failed or says "network / CORS." What do I do? This usually means the browser couldn't reach OpenRouter directly. Try the Use my own AI option (copy the prompt into ChatGPT/Claude/Gemini, paste the result back), or use the desktop app , which makes the request without that browser restriction. It says to add a key in Settings. You need an OpenRouter API key to use automatic generation. Setup steps are on Adding Cards — or skip the key and use your own AI chat. Desktop app The installer warns about an "unidentified developer." The desktop apps are unsigned. On macOS , right-click the app → Open . On Windows , click More info → Run anyway . This is expected and safe to proceed. Still stuck? Open an issue on the GitHub repository .

docs.text: Getting Started This page takes you from never having seen Runico to your first study session. No prior experience needed — if you can use a web browser, you can use Runico. Step 1 — Open Runico You have two choices. For most people, the browser is the easiest start. Option A: In your web browser (recommended) Just click here: ▶ Open Runico Nothing to install, no account to make. It works in Chrome, Edge, Safari, Firefox, and most modern browsers. 💡 Tip: Chrome or Edge give you the smoothest experience because Runico can auto-save your work straight to a file. Other browsers work too — they just save by downloading the file. See Your Library File for the details. Option B: Download the desktop app Prefer a real app on your computer? Grab an installer for macOS, Windows, or Linux from the Releases page . ⚠️ The desktop apps are unsigned , so on first launch your system may warn about an "unidentified developer." That's expected. On macOS, right-click the app → Open ; on Windows, click More info → Run anyway . Step 2 — Create your library The first thing you'll see is the Start screen , asking you to open a library or create a new one. A library is simply the file that holds everything you make in Runico — all your folders, topics, cards, and study history, in one place. First time? Click New to create a fresh, empty library. Returning, or moving from another device? Click Open and pick your existing .runico file. 📁 Want to understand exactly what this file is, where it lives, and how to back it up? Read Your Library File . Step 3 — Put some cards in it A brand-new library is empty, so let's add something to study. The fastest way is to let Runico make cards for you : drop in a page of notes, a screenshot, or a textbook figure, and it drafts flashcards you can review and keep. 👉 Full walkthrough: Adding Cards (You can also write cards by hand, or paste cards you generated in your own AI chat like ChatGPT or Claude — both are covered on that page.) Step 4 — Study Open a topic and start practicing. For each card you: Recall the answer in your head. Reveal it to check yourself. Tap Continue to move on, or Pause to stop and save your place. That's the entire study loop — no "rate yourself 1–5" scales. 👉 Full walkthrough: Studying Cards Step 5 — Come back later Whenever you return, the home screen greets you with a Continue practice button that drops you right back onto the card you stopped on. Nothing to hunt for. 👉 See Resuming Your Last Session . Where to next? Organize what you've made → Organizing Your Library See if you're improving → Tracking Your Progress Make it look the way you like → Settings Stuck on something → FAQ & Troubleshooting

docs.text: 📚 Glossary New to flashcards or to Runico? Here's every term used in this wiki, in plain English. Card — a single flashcard. It shows a prompt with part of it hidden; you recall the hidden part, then reveal it to check. See Studying Cards . Cloze — a card style where a word or phrase in a sentence is blanked out for you to fill in. Example: "The {{nucleus}} holds the cell's DNA." ("Cloze" just means fill in the blank .) Q&A card — a card with a plain question on the front and the answer on the back. Reversible card — a term-and-definition pair you can study in both directions (term → definition and definition → term). Image-occlusion card — a card made from a picture with a label covered by a box; you recall what's hidden underneath. Great for diagrams. See Image-Occlusion Cards . Topic — a single deck of cards about one thing, like Photosynthesis . Topics live inside folders. Folder / subject — a container that groups topics (and other folders). Folders can nest as deep as you like. Biology is a folder; Cell Biology might be a folder inside it. Library — your whole collection: all folders, topics, cards, and history. It's saved as one .runico file you own. See Your Library File . Merge — folding one library into another: open one .runico file and pull a second one's folders, topics, cards, and history into it. Matching folders/topics combine; new ones are added. See Merging Libraries . Duplicate (card) — during a merge, an incoming card that lands in the same place and has the same content as one you already have. Runico asks you to keep both, edit it, or skip it. Session (or "sitting") — one round of studying. Runico records each session to chart your progress. Due — cards that are ready for you to review now. A topic might say "4 due" meaning four cards are waiting. Accuracy — for one session, the cards you got right ÷ the cards you reviewed, as a percentage. The progress chart plots this over time. Action card — the summary panel that opens when you tap a topic in the browser, with its stats and buttons ( Continue practice , Open cards , View progress …). See Browsing and Practicing . Draft — an AI-generated card that's waiting for you to review (Keep or Remove) before it joins the topic. See Adding Cards . OpenRouter — the service Runico uses to talk to AI models for drafting cards. You add a free API key from it in Settings . API key — a private password-like string that lets Runico use AI on your behalf. It stays on your device. Canvas / theme — the background color you study against: Light, Warm, or Dark. File System Access API — the browser feature (in Chrome and Edge) that lets Runico auto-save straight into your .runico file. Browsers without it save by downloading.

docs.text: New to flashcards or to Runico? Here's every term used in this wiki, in plain English. Card — a single flashcard. It shows a prompt with part of it hidden; you recall the hidden part, then reveal it to check. See Studying Cards . Cloze — a card style where a word or phrase in a sentence is blanked out for you to fill in. Example: "The {{nucleus}} holds the cell's DNA." ("Cloze" just means fill in the blank .) Q&A card — a card with a plain question on the front and the answer on the back. Reversible card — a term-and-definition pair you can study in both directions (term → definition and definition → term). Image-occlusion card — a card made from a picture with a label covered by a box; you recall what's hidden underneath. Great for diagrams. See Image-Occlusion Cards . Topic — a single deck of cards about one thing, like Photosynthesis . Topics live inside folders. Folder / subject — a container that groups topics (and other folders). Folders can nest as deep as you like. Biology is a folder; Cell Biology might be a folder inside it. Library — your whole collection: all folders, topics, cards, and history. It's saved as one .runico file you own. See Your Library File . Merge — folding one library into another: open one .runico file and pull a second one's folders, topics, cards, and history into it. Matching folders/topics combine; new ones are added. See Merging Libraries . Duplicate (card) — during a merge, an incoming card that lands in the same place and has the same content as one you already have. Runico asks you to keep both, edit it, or skip it. Session (or "sitting") — one round of studying. Runico records each session to chart your progress. Due — cards that are ready for you to review now. A topic might say "4 due" meaning four cards are waiting. Accuracy — for one session, the cards you got right ÷ the cards you reviewed, as a percentage. The progress chart plots this over time. Action card — the summary panel that opens when you tap a topic in the browser, with its stats and buttons ( Continue practice , Open cards , View progress …). See Browsing and Practicing . Draft — an AI-generated card that's waiting for you to review (Keep or Remove) before it joins the topic. See Adding Cards . docs.text: 🖼️ Image-Occlusion Cards Draw, drag, and resize boxes over a figure to build image-occlusion cards. Looping demo · plays automatically Image occlusion is a fancy name for a simple idea: take a picture, cover up a label with a box, and study by recalling what's hidden. It's perfect for diagrams, anatomy, maps, and any labelled image — anything where where something is matters as much as what it is. Each box you draw becomes one card that hides that one label. How to mask an image Open the figure in the occlusion editor (you get here while adding cards from an image). Draw a box: click-and-drag across the part you want to hide. A mask box appears. Reposition: drag the middle of a box to move it. Resize: pull a corner handle to make it bigger or smaller. Delete: click the × on a box to remove it — keep only what matters. An on-screen hint reminds you: "Drag to add · drag to move · pull the corner to resize," and a live counter shows how many regions you've made ( 1 region, 2 regions, … ). 📱 On phones and tablets the corner handles and the × are enlarged so you can grab them with a finger. A worked example You have a diagram of a plant cell labelled Nucleus, Mitochondrion, Chloroplast, Cell wall . You: Draw a box over the word Nucleus . Draw boxes over the other three labels too — four boxes, four regions. That's four occlusion cards . When you study, Runico shows the diagram with one label hidden and asks "What's the highlighted region?" You recall it, then reveal to check. How you study them Occlusion cards work just like text cards in a session: Runico shows the picture with one box covering a label and asks "What's the highlighted region?" You recall the answer, then reveal what's underneath. Continue or Pause, same as always. See Studying Cards . Don't want to draw boxes yourself? If you're using the Use my own AI flow, there's an optional prompt that asks your AI chat to identify the labels on a figure for you — paste its response and Runico builds the occlusion card automatically. Related ✨ Adding Cards — where image cards are created. 📖 Studying Cards — how you practice them.

docs.text: 🔀 Merging Libraries Have study material in more than one .runico file? Fold one into the other — folders, topics, cards, and your study history come along, with a quick step to sort out any duplicates. Each Runico library is a self-contained .runico file . That's great for keeping things tidy, but sometimes you end up with two: a deck a classmate shared, a subject you started on another laptop, an export you want to fold back in. Merge brings them together. The short version Open the library you want to keep working in (call it A ). Click 🔀 Merge in the top bar and pick the second file ( B ). If any cards clash, resolve them (keep both / edit / skip). Done — B's content is now part of A, and A is saved automatically. 🔒 B is never touched. Merging only adds to the library you have open. The file you merged from is left exactly as it was on disk. How content is placed Runico doesn't just dump B's folders next to A's — it matches them up by name so things land where you'd expect: A folder or topic in B that has the same name in the same place as one in A is merged into it. Two folders both called Biology become one Biology . Anything in B that doesn't exist in A is added — a new topic appears inside the matching folder; a brand-new top-level folder appears alongside your existing ones. Everything that comes across gets fresh internal ids, so nothing collides with what you already had. Example. You merge Study Set B into your library: Your library (A) Merging in (B) Result ───────────────── ─────────────── ────────────────── Biology Biology ──▶ Biology (merged) └ Cell Biology └ Cell Biology ──▶ └ Cell Biology (cards combined) History └ Microbiology ──▶ └ Microbiology (added) Spanish ──▶ History └ Verbs ──▶ Spanish (added) └ Verbs Your study history comes too When a topic from B merges into one of yours, its progress isn't thrown away: Past sessions from both files are combined, so the topic's accuracy chart shows your whole history. Per-card pass/fail records travel with each card. Newly added folders and topics bring their own history with them. Resolving duplicate cards A card counts as a duplicate only when it lands in the same place and has the same content as a card you already have. (A card with the same wording under a different topic is not a duplicate — it's legitimately separate.) When duplicates turn up, Runico pauses and shows them to you one at a time, side by side — your copy on the left, the incoming copy on the right — so you can compare and choose: Action What happens Keep both The incoming card is added too. You end up with both copies. Edit Tweak the incoming card before it's added, so it's no longer a duplicate. Skip Keep only the copy you already had. The incoming card's study history is merged into your surviving card, so you don't lose its practice record. 💡 Nothing is ever discarded silently — if there's a duplicate, you decide what happens to it. If a merge finds no duplicates, it just completes, no questions asked. When it's done you'll see a short summary: "Added 1 folder, 2 topics, and 6 cards · 1 duplicate resolved." Merging three or more files Merge handles two files at a time, but it's repeatable — open A, merge B, then merge C, and so on. Each merge folds the next file into the growing library. Good to know Where the result goes. The merged result is written into the library you have open (A) and auto-saved to its file. There's no separate "save as" step. Changed your mind mid-merge? Use Cancel merge on the duplicate screen (or the Back arrow) — nothing is written until you finish resolving. No undo for a whole merge, but since B is untouched and the added content is easy to spot, you can always delete a grafted folder (it goes to the Trash ) or re-open B. What doesn't come across: B's trash and its unreviewed AI drafts are left behind, and your own "last session" marker is kept — only real library content is merged. A worked example You've been studying biology.runico all semester. A classmate sends you shared-deck.runico with extra cell-biology cards and a whole new Spanish set. You Open biology.runico . You click 🔀 Merge and pick shared-deck.runico . Their Cell Biology merges into yours; the new cards appear, and one card you both had ("Powerhouse of the cell?") shows up as a duplicate. You tap Skip — your version stays, and your classmate's practice history on it merges into yours. Their Spanish folder lands alongside your subjects. The summary reads "Added 1 folder, 1 topic, and 9 cards · 1 duplicate resolved," and biology.runico saves automatically. ✨ Related 💾 Your Library File — what a .runico file is and how saving works. 🌱 Organizing Your Library — move, copy, and sort after merging. 🗑️ Trash and Recovery — remove something a merge added. 📈 Tracking Your Progress — the history that merging preserves.

docs.text: 🌱 Organizing Your Library As your library grows, you'll want to tidy it up — group related topics, rename things, and move them around. All of this happens right inside the column browser . This page walks through each action. 💡 Folders nest as deep as you like , and topics (your decks of cards) live inside the deepest folder. There's no limit — organize it however makes sense to you. Make a new folder Open the folder you want the new folder to live inside (or stay at Everything for a top-level folder). At the bottom of the column, click ➕ New folder . Type a name (e.g. Microbiology ) and press Enter . Example: inside Biology , make a folder called Genetics to hold all your genetics topics. Rename a folder or topic Hover over the row (on touch devices the buttons are always visible) and tap the ⋯ More menu. Choose Rename . Type the new name and press Enter . Move something (Cut → Paste) Use this to relocate a folder or topic to a different place. Open the ⋯ More menu on the item → choose Cut . Navigate to the folder where you want it. Click Paste here at the bottom of that column. The item moves — it leaves its old home and lands in the new one. Example: you made Photosynthesis in the wrong place. Cut it, open Cell Biology , and Paste here. Copy something (Copy → Paste) Use this to duplicate a folder or topic. Open the ⋯ More menu → choose Copy . Navigate to the destination folder. Click Paste here . The original stays put and a duplicate (named with a "(copy)" suffix) appears in the new location. Copying a folder copies everything inside it too. ↔️ You can also drag and drop a folder or topic straight onto another folder to move it. Sort a column Each column has a Sort by control so you can order its contents however you like: Sort option Orders by Name (A–Z) Alphabetical, ascending (the default) Name (Z–A) Alphabetical, descending Most cards Fullest topics/folders first Manual The order you added them in Sorting only changes how things are displayed — it never moves or changes your actual cards. Delete something Open the ⋯ More menu → choose Delete . Confirm. ♻️ Deleting is safe. Folders, topics, and cards go to the Trash first, so you can restore them if you change your mind. Nothing is gone for good until you empty the trash. Add cards to a topic At the bottom of a column you'll also find ➕ Add AI Cards , which jumps straight into the card-creation flow for that location. See Adding Cards . Related 🗂️ Browsing and Practicing — getting around the library. 🔀 Merging Libraries — combine two .runico files into one. 🗑️ Trash and Recovery — undo a delete. ✨ Adding Cards — fill a topic with cards.

docs.text: ⏯️ Resuming Your Last Session The home view remembers your last session and shows how many cards remain. Looping demo · plays automatically You almost never finish everything in one sitting — and you shouldn't have to. Runico remembers the practice you were last in and greets you with it the moment you open the app. Getting back into study is a single tap , with no hunting for where you stopped. How it works You pause (or close the app) partway through a deck. Next time you open Runico , the home screen shows a big quick-resume button at the top. You tap it , and you're dropped straight back onto the exact card you left off on. The button changes to match where you are The quick-resume button is smart — it reads the state of your last session and relabels itself: Your situation The button says And shows, for example You paused partway through Continue practice Photosynthesis · 3 cards remaining You finished the whole deck Restart practice Photosynthesis You haven't started this one yet Begin practice Membrane transport So a glance tells you both what you were studying and how much is left. A worked example You study Photosynthesis during your lunch break and get through 5 of 8 cards, then tap Pause . That evening you open Runico. The home screen shows: ▶ Continue practice — Photosynthesis · 3 cards remaining One tap and you're back on card 6. No menus, no searching. Tips You don't have to use it. You can always ignore the button and browse to a different topic instead. Each topic remembers its own place too — open any topic in the browser and it shows "Paused at card 2 · 3 of 4 remaining" with its own resume action. Related 📖 Studying Cards — the study loop itself. 🗂️ Browsing and Practicing — start a different topic.

docs.text: 🔎 Seeing the Source Open the original material as a book page, with the studied term highlighted in context. Looping demo · plays automatically A flashcard on its own can feel like a fact floating in space. Runico fixes that: every card remembers where it came from. One tap opens the original material — the page, figure, or notes the card was made from — with the term you're studying highlighted right where it appears . It's context, not just a flashcard. How to see a card's source While studying (or reviewing), look at the card footer . Tap the Source link (it's labelled with where the card came from, e.g. Cell Biology · Ch. 4 ). The source opens like a book page — flowing prose around any figure — with your studied term highlighted inline. What it looks like A heading like "From this source · Eukaryotic cell · Ch. 4." Readable, textbook-style prose, with the studied term (e.g. nucleus ) highlighted in place so your eye goes straight to it. Any embedded figure, with its caption (e.g. Figure 4.3 — A generalized eukaryotic cell. Highlighted: Nucleus ). Why it's useful Understand, don't just memorize. Seeing the surrounding sentence helps the fact stick because it's connected to meaning. Settle a doubt instantly. Not sure why an answer is what it is? The source is one tap away — no leaving the app to dig through your notes. Trust your cards. You can always check a card against the material it was drafted from. A worked example You're studying a card about the nucleus and think "wait, is that the same as the nucleolus?" You tap Source , the original page opens with nucleus highlighted in a full sentence, and the distinction is clear in two seconds. You tap back and keep going. Related 📖 Studying Cards — where the Source link appears. ✨ Adding Cards — sources are captured when cards are created.

docs.text: 🎨 Settings Choose a canvas theme and the language for your cards and interface. Looping demo · plays automatically Open Settings (the ⚙️ gear icon in the top bar) to make Runico yours. There are three things to set: the look , the language , and your AI key . Appearance — pick a canvas to learn against The "canvas" is the background color you study on. Pick whatever's easiest on your eyes: Theme Canvas Best for Light White Bright rooms, daytime study — calm and clean. Warm Off-white All-day reading — softer, easier on the eye. Dark Near-black Low light and night-time study. The change is instant — tap a theme and the whole app switches. Language — for the interface and your cards Runico's interface is available in 7 languages : English Português (Brasil) — Portuguese (Brazil) Português (Portugal) — Portuguese (Portugal) Español — Spanish Русский — Russian Italiano — Italian 中文 — Chinese 📝 Important: this sets the language of the interface (buttons, labels). Your cards stay in whatever language you wrote them in. Switching to Spanish won't translate your existing flashcards — it just changes the app's own text. AI card generation — your OpenRouter key This is where you set up automatic card drafting. Under AI card generation you can: Paste and Save your OpenRouter API key (and Test key to confirm it works). Clear the key whenever you like. Choose a Generation model , with a rough "≈ N cards per $1" estimate to help you weigh quality vs. cost. 🔒 Your key is private: stored only on your device in the browser, or encrypted in your OS keychain in the desktop app. Full setup, model choice, and costs are on Using OpenRouter — or skip the key and use your own AI . A worked example It's late and your eyes are tired. You open Settings , tap Dark , and the canvas turns near-black instantly. Much better. You tap Done and get back to studying. 🌙 Related 🤖 Using OpenRouter — full key setup, model choice, and costs. ✨ Adding Cards — the AI card-drafting flow. ❓ FAQ & Troubleshooting — answers to common questions.

docs.text: 📖 Studying Cards Recall the answer, reveal it, then Continue or Pause — no grading scales to fuss over. Looping demo · plays automatically Studying in Runico is deliberately simple. A card shows you a prompt with the key idea hidden. You try to recall it from memory, reveal the answer to check yourself, and then make one of two choices . There is no "how well did you remember it, 1 to 5?" scale to agonize over. How a study session works A card appears with the key idea blanked out. Try to recall it before you reveal — that little moment of effort is what makes flashcards work. Tap Show answer to uncover the hidden part and check yourself. Now you have two buttons: - Continue → you're done with this card; move to the next one. - Pause → stop the session. Runico saves the exact card you're on so you can come back later. Repeat until the deck is finished (or you pause). That's the whole loop. 🎉 A worked example Say you're studying cell biology. A card shows: The [ ] contains the cell's genetic material. You think: "nucleus." You tap Show answer : The nucleus contains the cell's genetic material. You got it right, so you tap Continue . On to the next card. What you'll see on screen The prompt , with the answer blanked out (this style of card is called a cloze — see the Glossary ). A progress marker like 1 of 4 , so you always know how far along you are. Before revealing: a big Show answer button (and a quiet Pause ). After revealing: the Pause and Continue buttons. A footer with a Source link — tap it to read the original material this card came from. Two kinds of cards you'll study Text cards — a prompt with the answer hidden, exactly like the example above. Image-occlusion cards — a picture with a label box covered up. Instead of "Show answer," you reveal what's under the box. Great for diagrams and anatomy. See Image-Occlusion Cards . Keyboard shortcuts (desktop) On a computer with a keyboard, you can fly through a session without the mouse: Key Does Space Show the answer Enter Continue (got it) X Mark missed Esc Pause 📱 On phones and tablets these shortcuts don't show up — you just tap the on-screen buttons instead. Tips for beginners Be honest with yourself. Reveal after you've made a real guess, not before. Short and often beats long and rare. A few minutes a day is plenty. Don't worry about "missing" a card. It just means you'll see it again — that's the point. Related ⏯️ Resuming Your Last Session — pick up exactly where you paused. 📈 Tracking Your Progress — see if a topic is improving. 🔎 Seeing the Source — read where a card came from.

docs.text: 📈 Tracking Your Progress See whether a topic is trending up — per-session accuracy over time, with a drill-down to any single card. Looping demo · plays automatically Every time you study, Runico quietly records how you did. Track your progress turns that into a simple picture so you can answer one question: "Is this topic actually getting better?" ✅ Reporting only. Looking at your progress never changes what's due or how cards are scheduled. It's a mirror, not a control. What the chart shows The headline is per-session accuracy — the cards you got right ÷ the cards you reviewed in that one sitting. So if you reviewed 20 cards and got 17 right, that session plots at 85% . Bottom of the chart: time. Side of the chart: accuracy, from 0% to 100% . Each point: one study session, with a tooltip like 17/20 passed · May 12 . No scores, no jargon, no mysterious "retention percentage" — just your raw pass rate, session by session. How to open it There are two ways in: From the browser: tap View progress on a topic's action card . From the cards screen: open a topic's Open cards view — the little accuracy-trend banner at the top doubles as a View full performance → link. Reading the numbers Below the chart, a stats row reads out: Latest sitting — your most recent session's accuracy, with a ▲ / ▼ showing whether it went up or down versus the session before. Window average — the average across the dates currently shown. Sittings — how many sessions are in view. Paging back through time The chart shows 30 days at a time , starting with the most recent. Use Earlier / Later 30 days to page through your whole history. The visible date range is labelled right under the chart. A span with no study sessions just shows a quiet "nothing here yet" state. Drilling into a single card Want to know which card keeps tripping you up? Below the chart, every card is listed with a mini sparkline and its pass rate. Tap a card — the chart switches to show that one card's history: a green dot for each session you passed it, a red dot for each miss. Tap Deck overview to return to the whole-topic trend. A worked example You've studied Photosynthesis six times over three weeks. You open its progress and see the line climbing from 60% → 75% → 90%. The stats row shows Latest: 90% ▲ . You scroll down, spot one card stuck at lots of red misses, tap it, and confirm it's the one term you keep forgetting. Now you know exactly what to focus on. 🎯 Related 📖 Studying Cards — the sessions that feed this chart. 🗂️ Browsing and Practicing — where the View progress button lives.

docs.text: 🗑️ Trash and Recovery Deleted something you didn't mean to? Don't panic. In Runico, deleting a folder, topic, or card doesn't destroy it — it goes to the Trash first, where you can restore it. How deleting works When you delete a folder , topic , or card , it's moved to the trash instead of being erased. It stays there until you decide to remove it for good. Nothing expires or disappears on its own. Opening the trash Click the 🗑️ Trash button in the top bar. You'll see everything you've deleted, each tagged with its kind — Folder , Topic , or Card . (If you haven't deleted anything, you'll just see "The trash is empty." ) Restoring something Find the item in the trash. Click Restore . Confirm — it returns to its original location . ⚠️ One catch with cards: to restore a card , its topic must still exist. If you deleted the whole topic too, restore the topic first , then the card. Deleting for good When you're sure you don't need something: Delete forever on a single item removes just that one (this can't be undone). Empty trash removes everything in the trash at once. Both ask you to confirm first, because these are the only actions in Runico that truly can't be reversed. A worked example You meant to delete one stale card but deleted the whole Mitosis topic by mistake. You: Open the Trash . Find Mitosis (tagged Topic ) and click Restore . It pops back into Cell Biology , right where it was. Crisis averted. 😌 Related 🌱 Organizing Your Library — where deleting happens. 💾 Your Library File — the trash is saved inside your library file too.

docs.text: 🤖 Using OpenRouter Connect Runico to an AI model so it can draft cards for you from a page, a screenshot, or your notes. Setup takes about a minute, and you only pay for what you use. This page covers the OpenRouter setup in depth. For the card-drafting flow itself, see Adding Cards . No key, or don't want one? You can draft cards for free with your own AI chat instead. What is OpenRouter? OpenRouter is a service that gives you access to many AI models (from Google, OpenAI, Anthropic, DeepSeek, Mistral, and others) through a single account and a single API key . Runico uses it as the bridge to whichever model you choose — so you're never locked into one provider, and you can pick a cheaper or more capable model whenever you like. Runico has no AI server of its own . When you generate cards, the request goes straight from your device to OpenRouter using your key, and you're billed by OpenRouter for that usage. Setting up your key Create an account at openrouter.ai (free). Create an API key in your OpenRouter dashboard, and copy it. In Runico, open Settings (the ⚙️ gear) → AI card generation . Paste your key and click Save . Click Test key to confirm Runico can reach OpenRouter with it. ✅ You can Clear the key at any time from the same place. 🔑 You'll need a little credit on your OpenRouter account to generate cards — add a small amount in their dashboard. Most models cost a fraction of a cent per batch of cards (see below). Choosing a generation model Under AI card generation → Generation model , Runico offers a curated list of models, ordered cheapest → most capable . Next to each one it shows a rough estimate: ≈ N cards per $1 …so you can balance quality against cost at a glance. A cheaper model drafts more cards per dollar; a stronger model may write better cards from messy or complex material. You can switch models any time — the choice is saved as one of your app preferences. 💡 Tip: start with one of the cheaper models. If the drafts from a tricky source feel thin, bump up to a more capable one for that material. Where your key is stored (and kept private) Your key is never sent anywhere except OpenRouter, and it's stored differently depending on where you run Runico: Where you run Runico Where the key lives Browser (Chrome, Edge, …) On your device only, in local storage. It never leaves your machine except in the request to OpenRouter. Desktop app Encrypted in your operating-system keychain (macOS Keychain, Windows Credential Manager, etc.). It never reaches the page at all. Because the key is a device preference (like your theme), it's not part of your .runico library file . If you open your library on another device, you'll re-enter the key there once. Browser vs. desktop: a note on reliability In the desktop app , the generation request is made by the app itself, so it works without browser cross-origin (CORS) restrictions and tends to "just work." In the browser , the request goes directly from the page to OpenRouter. This works in most modern browsers, but if you ever hit a network / CORS error, you have two easy fallbacks: Use the desktop app , or Switch to Use my own AI — no key or network call from Runico at all. What it costs Runico itself is free. The only thing that can cost money is AI generation, billed by OpenRouter based on the model you pick and how much you generate. The "≈ N cards per $1" estimate next to each model is your guide. Typical card batches cost cents. Prefer zero cost? Use your own AI chat is completely free. Troubleshooting "Add a key in Settings." You haven't saved an OpenRouter key yet, or it was cleared. Follow Setting up your key above — or skip the key and use your own AI . "Test key" fails / generation says the key is invalid. Double-check you copied the whole key, that it's still active in your OpenRouter dashboard, and that your account has a little credit. Generation says "network" or "CORS." The browser couldn't reach OpenRouter directly. Try the desktop app , or use Use my own AI . Generation says "rate." OpenRouter is rate-limiting the requests; wait a moment and try again, or pick a different model. Related ✨ Adding Cards — the full card-drafting flow. 💬 Using Your Own AI — draft cards for free with ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini. 🎨 Settings — where the key and model live. ❓ FAQ & Troubleshooting — more common fixes.

docs.text: 💬 Using Your Own AI No OpenRouter key? No problem. Runico can hand you a ready-made prompt to run in any AI chat you already use — ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini — then turn the reply back into cards. It's completely free and needs no key and no network access from Runico. This is the "Use my own AI" path on the Add screen. For the automatic alternative, see Using OpenRouter ; for the whole card-drafting picture, see Adding Cards . When to use this You don't have (or don't want) an OpenRouter key. You'd rather not pay per generation — your existing AI chat subscription already covers it. Automatic generation in the browser hit a network / CORS error. You simply prefer to see and tweak the prompt yourself. How it works Runico does the hard part — it writes a precise prompt and knows how to read the reply. You just shuttle text between Runico and your chat assistant. Start adding cards. In the browser , open a folder and begin adding cards to a topic (name it when asked, and drop in your source if you have one). Choose Use my own AI . Instead of running generation itself, Runico shows a ready-made prompt . Copy the prompt. Click Copy prompt . Paste it into your AI chat — ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, anything. If your source is an image (a diagram or screenshot), attach that image in the chat alongside the prompt. Copy the reply. The assistant responds with card data; copy its whole response. Paste it back into Runico and click Use these cards . Review the drafts. Just like the automatic flow, you get Keep , Edit , or Remove for each card. Keep the good ones. ✨ 💡 The prompt is carefully written so the reply is in exactly the format Runico expects — so copy the assistant's response as-is , without editing it, and let Runico parse it. Image-occlusion cards from your own AI If your source is a labeled diagram , Runico can also build an image-occlusion card (one that hides the labels for you to recall). There's an optional second prompt for this: Copy the occlusion prompt as well. Paste it into your chat with the image attached . Paste that reply back into Runico too. Runico crops the figure and places the masks for you, ready to fine-tune in draft review. Either prompt alone is enough — whatever you paste back is combined into one set of drafts. Tips Any assistant works — there's nothing OpenRouter-specific here. Use whichever chat you like best. Attach the image when your source is a picture, or the assistant can only work from text. Don't reformat the reply. Paste the assistant's answer exactly as given; Runico is built to read that format. If a reply doesn't import, ask the assistant to "return only the requested format," then copy and paste again. A worked example You're on Safari (no auto-generation) and have a ChatGPT tab open. In Runico you start adding cards to Cell signaling and choose Use my own AI . You Copy prompt , switch to ChatGPT, paste it, and hit enter. ChatGPT replies with a block of card data. You copy the whole thing. Back in Runico you paste it and click Use these cards — six drafts appear. You Keep five and Remove one. Done, for free. 🎉 Related ✨ Adding Cards — all three ways to get cards into a topic. 🤖 Using OpenRouter — the automatic, in-app alternative. 🖼️ Image-Occlusion Cards — masked-figure cards in depth.

Keep the good ones. ✨ 💡 The prompt is carefully written so the reply is in exactly the format Runico expects — so copy the assistant's response as-is , without editing it, and let Runico parse it.
docs.title: How it works

docs.text: If your source is a labeled diagram , Runico can also build an image-occlusion card (one that hides the labels for you to recall). There's an optional second prompt for this: Copy the occlusion prompt as well. Paste it into your chat with the image attached . Paste that reply back into Runico too. Runico crops the figure and places the masks for you, ready to fine-tune in draft review. Either prompt alone is enough — whatever you paste back is combined into one set of drafts.
docs.title: Image-occlusion cards from your own AI

docs.text: Any assistant works — there's nothing OpenRouter-specific here. Use whichever chat you like best. Attach the image when your source is a picture, or the assistant can only work from text. Don't reformat the reply. Paste the assistant's answer exactly as given; Runico is built to read that format. If a reply doesn't import, ask the assistant to "return only the requested format," then copy and paste again.
docs.title: Tips

docs.text: You're on Safari (no auto-generation) and have a ChatGPT tab open. In Runico you start adding cards to Cell signaling and choose Use my own AI . You Copy prompt , switch to ChatGPT, paste it, and hit enter. ChatGPT replies with a block of card data. You copy the whole thing. Back in Runico you paste it and click Use these cards — six drafts appear. You Keep five and Remove one. Done, for free. 🎉

docs.text: 💾 Your Library File Everything you make in Runico — folders, topics, cards, study history, trash, and your last session — lives in one file you own , with a .runico extension. This page explains what it is, where it lives, and how to keep it safe. It's the one "plumbing" concept worth understanding, and it's simple. What is it? Think of the .runico file as your notebook . Just like a Word document holds your writing, a .runico file holds your entire Runico library. Open the file and you're back exactly where you left off. You can have as many libraries as you want — for example one for school and one for a hobby — each in its own file. Creating and opening it On the Start screen (the first thing you see): New — creates a fresh, empty library. Open — opens an existing .runico file you (or another device) saved earlier. How saving works This is the one place the browser and the desktop app differ: Where you're running How it saves Chrome / Edge (browser) Auto-saves straight back into your file as you work. The top bar shows Saving… → Saved . Other browsers (Safari, Firefox…) Saving downloads a fresh copy of the file. You'll see a note about this on the Start screen. Desktop app Auto-saves natively to your file, just like Chrome/Edge. 💡 Why the difference? Auto-save-in-place relies on a browser capability (the File System Access API) that only some browsers have. If yours doesn't, nothing is lost — you just save by downloading, then keep using that downloaded file. Backing up and syncing Because your library is just a normal file, you're in full control: Back it up by copying the .runico file somewhere safe. Sync between computers by keeping it in a cloud folder (Dropbox, iCloud Drive, Google Drive…) and Open -ing it on each device. Move between browser and desktop by opening the same file in both. Where do my preferences live? Your theme , language , AI model choice , and OpenRouter key are not part of the library file — they're settings for the app itself: In the browser: stored on your device (under a runico:v3: prefix in local storage). In the desktop app: in an app-managed settings file, with the OpenRouter key encrypted in your OS keychain. So if you move your .runico file to a new device, you'll re-pick your theme and re-enter your key there — but all your cards and history come along in the file. A worked example You study on your laptop in Chrome (auto-save just works). You keep school.runico in your iCloud Drive folder. On the bus you open the same file on your phone, study a few cards, and it saves back. Tonight on your desktop you Open it again — every card and your exact stopping point are right there. 📚 Related 🚀 Getting Started — creating your first library. 🗑️ Trash and Recovery — undeleting things inside a library. ❓ FAQ & Troubleshooting — "where is my data?" and privacy.
